summary:
The Fall of Assyria (614–609 BC) was the rapid and total collapse of what had been the world's greatest military empire — the Neo-Assyrian state that had dominated the Near East for over two centuries — destroyed by a coalition of Babylonians under Nabopolassar and Medes under Cyaxares. Assur, the religious capital, fell in 614 BC; the great metropolis of Nineveh (see [[event:1903|Fall of Nineveh]]) was sacked in 612 BC; and the last Assyrian remnant was destroyed at Harran in 609 BC. The swiftness and completeness of the collapse astonished the ancient world.

The reasons for Assyria's sudden fall remain debated by historians. The empire had been severely weakened by a prolonged civil war in the 650s BC between King Ashurbanipal and his brother who controlled Babylon; decades of intensive military campaigns had drained manpower and resources; the empire's extreme reliance on terror had generated deep hatred among its subjects who joined or welcomed its destroyers; and the loss of the Levantine tribute zone to Babylonian control cut off significant revenues. The archaeological sites of Nineveh, Nimrud, and Assur were largely abandoned after the destruction, preserving their remains — including the libraries and palaces that 19th-century archaeologists excavated. The Neo-Babylonian Empire that succeeded Assyria inherited its administrative traditions while representing a very different cultural and religious orientation.
